dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a suspected overdose at the back steps of an apartment in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I don't remember much. The last thing I recall is drinking a beer and my friend came over. I don't know what happened.
A friend found you unresponsive. Were you aware of what was going on before things went fuzzy?
His pupils were initially pinpoint-about 1 mm each but still reactive.
Blood glucose is 167 mg/dL. It's on the higher side but not outside the normal range.
The EKG shows sinus rhythm, and it was the same prior to Narcan-no arrhythmias noted so far.
I take Inderal every day for my high blood pressure. Tylenol if I need pain, and some sleep medication like Remeron or Zyrtec.
Before Narcan, his pulse was 90 beats per minute, and post-Narcan, it dropped to 68 beats per minute.
Pre-Narcan, his respiratory rate was 6-very slow but regular and unlabored.
His initial oxygen saturation was 86%. We increased the oxygen with a nasal cannula at 6 LPM and were able to bring it up to 99%.
After Narcan, he regained full range of motion in all four limbs-no signs of restriction or movement issues.
I'll start a 4-lead ECG monitoring as part of our protocol. It helps detect any arrhythmias during transport.
We'll place an 18G IV in his left forearm as per Standing Order. A saline lock was attached-standard protocol for this type of case.
This is protocol 7-1-medical overdose/poisoning-opioid. Naloxone has been administered. We performed a two-provider medication cross-check on the dose.
Following Narcan, his pupils improved to 3mm and became reactive. His respiratory rate increased to 12-16, and his oxygen saturation reached 99%.
His mental status improved to GCS 15. He is now alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-AOX4.
Blood pressure post-Narcan is 128/46, pulse has dropped to 68. We'll keep him on supplemental oxygen via a nasal cannula at 6 LPM.
We monitored end-tidal CO2 and saw a decrease from the 60s to the 40s after Narcan. That's a sign of improved breathing.
We'll use five-point seatbelt restraints for transport. It's standard protocol for this situation to ensure your safety during the transfer.
He has a known medical history of CV-Primary Hypertension, and we'll continue monitoring this. His current medications include cetirizine for allergies and mirtazapine for sleep.
I also take mirtazapine at night, but I don't think I took anything else today.
The patient doesn't have any pain at this time-his pain level is 0.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how would you rate any pain you may be experiencing?
Can you tell me the name of this place and what the year is?
You responded well to voice now, which is an indicator of improved consciousness.
My initial spO2 and pressure readings are trending more towards normal than away. No sign of acute distress or heartbeat irregularities.
I'll reassess his breathing and IV site in a minute to ensure nothing changes unexpectedly.
When was the last time you had something to drink?
Based on his GCS, pinpoint pupils, and respiratory depression, this fits the opioid overdose protocol-medical 7-1.
We just documented the Narcan dose-0.1 mg IV-with proper cross-checking.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the closest appropriate facility with the appropriate staff to handle an opioid-related case.
He was already placed in a position of comfort and secured with five-point restraints.
What exactly happened right before you felt things start fading?
We'll monitor for any signs of skin changes or movement loss, but so far everything appears stable post-Narcan.
Skin is still warm, dry, and normal in color-no signs of rashes.
He moved symmetrically in all extremities prior to being securely loaded for transport.
Pre-care and transport documentation is all updated in real-time with active monitoring status.
We'll run a 12-lead EKG in the ambulance once we captain the gurney to check for any acute cardiac changes.
We're going to continue ECG and vital monitoring if he remains stable.
We'll roll him out gently and stay connected during transport-meeting the hospital on arrival.
No drug allergies reported-on file per patient's sister, so Narcan was safe to give.
We've contacted the ER at [COMPANY_NAME]-preliminary details en route.
He's lying flat and comfortable on the gurney with oxygen on and IV secured.
The ER team will get the full scoop when we arrive.
He's now in position with five-point restraints and monitoring is stable once we enter the vehicle.
The IV is now a saline lock in case more meds are needed during transport.
He did express a desire for first responders to contact him for recovery support, so we'll follow up on that.
He's doing better, and we'll be in transport in a few with continued flow and monitoring.
All field information is being compiled per standard for hospital and our internal review.
Care has now been officially transferred to the ER team.
